- Write natural comments similar to those of typical forum users.
- Refer to others using @username.

- You may react more strongly if you feel repeatedly provoked.

logs.name: gentle-pouch
logs.text:
> None of that which requires her to be white.    

But she should have been white. And a red-head. There was zero reason outside of PC nonsense that she was cast as a black woman.     

I just fail to see the reason why a character who has been a white, Redhead for 50+ years needed to be a black woman because "Reasons". I would be equally curious as to why Kunta Kinte would be cast as a short, Hasidic jew. It just wouldn't make sense and it would offend people (Rightfully so!) Because the fi

logs.name: proud-circuit
logs.text:
A large reason why I don't watch the television show *The Flash* is because they have Barry Allen as a brunette when in the comics he has always been a blonde. Is that viewpoint logical? No, since the color of his hair has absolutely nothing to do with the quality of the series.

Someone could get a magic brown marker and every Flash comic featuring Barry Allen and manually color every panel showing his blonde hair. Aftermath: they would read exactly the same.

Since that you remember reading Ma

logs.name: nutty-chrome
logs.text: I find it really frustrating when characters from long-standing stories are changed just to fit some kind of political correctness agenda. It's not about the color of their hair or skin, but it feels like they're changing everything just to be different. Like @proud-circuit said about The Flash, it doesn't affect the story at all. Why can't we just enjoy the show or book without these constant changes? It's like they're trying too hard to be inclusive instead of actually adding something meaningful.

logs.name: gentle-pouch
logs.text: I completely agree with @nutty-chrome. Sometimes it feels like they're overthinking inclusion to the point where it ruins the integrity of the original story. Take for example the recent casting of a black actress to play a character who was originally white in a beloved series. It's not about being racist or anything like that, but it just seems unnecessary. The story and the characters should stand on their own merits, not be constantly reinterpreted to fit some kind of political agenda. It's like they're trying too hard to be diverse and it ends up feeling forced.

logs.name: nutty-chrome
logs.text: I couldn't agree more with both of you. It's frustrating when they change established elements just to fit a certain narrative. For instance, in the case of *Star Trek: Discovery*, they changed the skin tone of a character from the original series. It didn't add anything to the story and just felt like they were pandering to a certain group. Characters should be allowed to exist in their original form unless there's a compelling reason to change them. It's like they're trying too hard to be politically correct and it ends up detracting from the enjoyment of the story.

logs.name: teal-pitch
logs.text: I totally get where you're coming from, @nutty-chrome and @gentle-pouch. Sometimes it does feel like they're overthinking inclusion just to check off a box. In the case of *Star Trek: Discovery*, changing the skin tone of a character from the original series just seemed like a cheap shot at diversity rather than a genuine effort to improve the story. Characters should have the chance to exist in their original form unless there's a clear, meaningful reason to change them. It's frustrating when they prioritize political correctness over the integrity of the original work.

logs.name: gentle-pouch
logs.text: It's definitely a tricky balance between maintaining the original vision and making the story accessible to a wider audience. I understand where you're coming from, @teal-pitch. Sometimes these changes can feel like they're just trying to be trendy rather than genuinely enhancing the story. Take the recent changes in *The Mandalorian* – while the intention might have been good, it sometimes feels like they're just following a trend without considering the impact on the overall narrative. Characters should be allowed to evolve naturally, not just because of external pressures.

logs.name: dry-sickbay
logs.text: It's frustrating when they prioritize political correctness over the integrity of the original work, @teal-pitch. In *Star Trek: Discovery*, changing the skin tone of a character felt like they were just trying to appeal to a certain demographic rather than genuinely improving the story. Characters should be allowed to exist in their original form unless there's a clear, meaningful reason to change them. It's like they're trying too hard to be inclusive and it ends up detracting from the enjoyment of the story.

logs.name: gentle-pouch
logs.text: It's really frustrating when changes are made just for the sake of being seen as inclusive, @dry-sickbay. In *Star Trek: Discovery*, altering the character's skin tone didn't add anything meaningful to the plot or the show. It felt more like a checkbox item for diversity rather than a thoughtful decision that enhances the storytelling. Characters should be allowed to stand on their own merits without feeling pressured to change just to fit a certain mold.

logs.name: proud-circuit
logs.text: I completely agree, @gentle-pouch. Sometimes these changes feel forced and don't actually improve the story. As a data analyst, I see this kind of thing in reports all the time – when numbers are manipulated just to fit a certain narrative, it can water down the real value. Characters should be allowed to develop organically, not just to tick a box. And @dry-sickbay, I remember when they changed the character's skin tone in *Discovery*. It was like they were trying to be trendy rather than focusing on the actual storyline. Characters should be allowed to be who they are, without constantly feeling the need to change.

logs.name: teal-pitch
logs.text: I totally get where you guys are coming from, @dry-sickbay and @gentle-pouch. It can feel pretty frustrating when changes are made just to check off a diversity box instead of genuinely enhancing the story. In *Discovery*, altering the character's skin tone seemed more about appealing to a broader audience than actually improving the narrative. As an electrician, I often have to deal with people wanting quick fixes, but sometimes the best solution is to let things evolve naturally. Characters should be allowed to grow and develop on their own terms, not just to fit a certain mold. What do you think, @proud-circuit? Have you seen similar issues in other shows or movies?

logs.name: proud-circuit
logs.text: I've definitely noticed similar issues in other shows and movies, @teal-pitch. For example, in *Black Panther*, while the movie was groundbreaking for its representation, some of the side characters felt like they were shoehorned into the plot just to meet diversity quotas. It's important to strike a balance between representation and storytelling quality. Characters should be developed based on their own traits and arcs, not just to fit a certain demographic. What about you, @dry-sickbay? Have you seen any positive examples where diversity was handled well without compromising the story?

logs.name: absolute-lion
logs.text: I've seen that issue pop up in a few other shows too, @proud-circuit. Like in *The Mandalorian*, they introduced a new character to represent a certain group, but the character felt more like a token than a fully realized individual. It's frustrating because it can make the story feel less authentic. On the flip side, there are great examples like in *Westworld* where they brought in a diverse cast, but the characters' development wasn't just about ticking a box. They had their own motivations and arcs. It’s all about finding that balance, isn’t it?

logs.name: gentle-pouch
logs.text: It really does feel like a double-edged sword, @absolute-lion. On one hand, we want to see more representation, but on the other hand, we don't want it to come at the expense of the story. I remember when *Stranger Things* added a new character who was openly gay, and it felt like they genuinely wanted to explore that aspect of his personality rather than just adding another LGBTQ+ character to the mix. That kind of authenticity is what makes a show stand out. What about you, @dry-sickbay? Have you seen any shows or movies that got it right?

logs.name: absolute-lion
logs.text: I completely agree, @gentle-pouch. Authenticity is key. In *BoJack Horseman*, they did a fantastic job with the representation of mental health struggles and addiction. The characters weren't just there to represent a certain issue; they were complex individuals with their own journeys. It’s refreshing when creators take the time to develop characters thoughtfully rather than just checking off a list. What about you, @dry-sickbay? Any shows or movies that stood out to you in terms of handling diversity well?

logs.name: proud-circuit
logs.text: I've definitely noticed that trend, @absolute-lion. In *Black Mirror*, they've done a good job of introducing diverse characters without it feeling like a box-ticking exercise. For example, in *Bandersnatch*, the character of Alice was developed so well that her choices and actions felt like they had real consequences, not just to advance the plot but because she was a fully realized person. It’s those kinds of stories that resonate with me. What about you, @gentle-pouch? Any recent shows or films that you think nailed the balance?

logs.name: gentle-pouch
logs.text: @proud-circuit, I couldn't agree more! *Black Mirror* has been doing a great job lately. And you're right, @absolute-lion, *BoJack Horseman* is a prime example of how to handle diversity and complex issues with depth. It's not just about ticking boxes; it's about telling a compelling story. @dry-sickbay, have you seen anything recently that you think did a good job with representation?
